critic_raw:
This is a carefully designed experiment. The temporal split, group-level incident assignment, preprocessing discipline, and sensitivity analysis address many standard failure modes. I will focus on issues with specific failure mechanisms rather than generic concerns.

**Issue 1: Analyst disposition history is structurally correlated with the label it is supposed to predict — not as leakage, but as a self-referential feedback loop that inflates NDCG without improving detection.**

The specific claim: analyst disposition history (e.g., fraction of alerts from the same rule confirmed malicious in the past 30 days) adds predictive signal beyond SIEM attributes.

The mechanism of failure: The label is derived from analyst ticket dispositions. The analyst history features are aggregates of prior analyst ticket dispositions from the same rule or analyst. If analysts have a systematic bias — for example, they over-triage rule X because of a high-profile incident in month 9 — then alerts from rule X will get high disposition rates in the history window, and the model will rank them highly, and they will also tend to get high labels (because the same biased analysts are labeling them). The model learns to replicate analyst bias, and the metric rewards it, because both the signal and the label share the same source. This is not temporal leakage — the time-ordering is correct — but it is circular signal: you are using past analyst judgment to predict future analyst judgment, evaluated against future analyst judgment. The sensitivity analysis described (holding analyst features constant) partially addresses this but doesn't resolve it: the improvement could be real (analysts learned something genuine) or artifactual (shared bias). Stratification by analyst partially helps but doesn't break the circularity if the same analyst population generates both features and labels across train and test.

Evidence one way or the other: If the improvement in NDCG@10 largely disappears on incidents triaged by analysts who were not active in the training period (e.g., new hires), that is strong evidence of bias amplification. If it persists, that suggests genuine signal. The experiment as described stratifies by analyst but does not specifically test this analyst-overlap confound.

**Issue 2: Incident-level label inheritance conceals within-incident ranking degeneracy and may make NDCG@10 nearly uninformative for the stated operational goal.**

The specific claim: NDCG@10 on incident-grouped alert lists reflects operational improvement in surfacing true-positive alerts.

The mechanism of failure: All alerts within an incident inherit the same disposition label. This means within a confirmed-malicious incident, all constituent alerts receive relevance=2 — regardless of which alert was actually the early indicator that mattered operationally. A model that surfaces any alert from a true-positive incident scores equally well as one that surfaces the specific early-indicator alert. Within an incident group, NDCG collapses to something close to group-level binary recall, not fine-grained alert prioritization. This directly undermines the stated business objective of reducing time-to-detection, which requires surfacing the *right* alert early — the one that most compresses detection time — not just any alert from a confirmed incident. If incident groups are small (e.g., 3–5 alerts each), NDCG@10 on such groups is nearly saturated by any model that correctly identifies incident membership, which the SIEM severity feature may already do. The metric would then measure something close to incident-level recall rather than within-incident prioritization quality.

Evidence one way or the other: Report the distribution of incident group sizes. If the median group size is below 10, NDCG@10 is effectively measuring whether any incident alert appears in the top-k, not fine-grained ranking. Additionally, measure whether the baseline already achieves near-ceiling NDCG@10 on small incident groups, which would indicate the metric has insufficient dynamic range to detect the improvement.

**Issue 3: The 5-fold temporal cross-validation within a 6-month validation window introduces a methodological inconsistency that may cause hyperparameter selection to overfit to short-term patterns not present in the test window.**

The specific claim: Hyperparameter tuning on the validation set with 5-fold temporal CV generalizes to the test set.

The mechanism of failure: The validation period is months 13–18. The 5-fold temporal CV within this period creates folds of roughly 5–6 weeks each. Hyperparameters are selected to maximize performance on these short-horizon folds. However, the test set covers months 19–24 — a full 6-month window following an 18-month training history. The model is then evaluated on a longer temporal horizon than the individual folds it was tuned on. If threat patterns have drift cycles longer than 5–6 weeks (which is common — e.g., seasonal phishing campaigns, quarterly patch cycles), hyperparameters selected to fit the short folds may not be optimal for 6-month generalization. This is not catastrophic but it means the tuning criterion and the evaluation criterion operate at different temporal scales.

Evidence one way or the other: Compare NDCG@10 on the test set with NDCG@10 on the last fold of the validation CV. If the gap is large and systematically directional (test is worse), that is evidence of hyperparameter-induced overfitting to the validation period's local patterns.

**Summary:**

Issue 1 is the most fundamental: the feedback loop between analyst history features and analyst-derived labels means the improvement metric cannot cleanly separate genuine threat signal from learned analyst behavior. The sensitivity analysis proposed is a good start but is insufficiently targeted to close this gap — specifically, it needs to test analyst-disjoint train/test splits.

Issue 2 questions whether NDCG@10 with uniform within-incident labels has enough sensitivity to measure the thing the business actually cares about. This could make the entire experiment incapable of detecting meaningful operational improvement even if it exists.

Issue 3 is real but lower-stakes — it affects tuning quality but not the validity of the comparison itself.

The experiment is well-constructed in many respects (temporal split discipline, preprocessing hygiene, scope honesty), but Issues 1 and 2 are structural and could cause the conclusions to be misleading.
all_issues_raised: Analyst disposition history features and labels share the same source (analyst judgment), creating a self-referential feedback loop that inflates NDCG without necessarily improving intrusion detection beyond learned analyst bias.

Uniform within-incident label inheritance means all alerts in a confirmed incident receive relevance=2, making NDCG@10 measure incident-level recall rather than fine-grained within-incident alert prioritization — which is the stated business objective.

5-fold temporal CV within the 6-month validation window tunes hyperparameters on 5–6 week horizons, which may not generalize to the 6-month test evaluation window if threat drift cycles are longer than individual folds.
